source_metadata.title_raw: Janet E. Coleson, Esq. - A-19-016 - March 22, 2019 - Walnut Creek

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Janet E. Coleson, Esq. 
Best Best & Krieger, LLP 
2001 N. Main Street, Suite 390 
Walnut Creek, CA 94596 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-016 
Dear Ms. Coleson: 
March 22, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act ("Act") 1 on behalf of Town of Fairfax Mayor Barbara Coler. Please note 
that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under 
other general conflict of interest prohibitions, such as common law conflict of interest or Section 
1090. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby ( 197 5) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not 
the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Does Mayor Coler have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act in participating in a 
Town Council decision to adopt an ordinance amending the Town's Zoning Code to set forth 
objective criteria for the Planned Development District ("PPD"), when she owns a townhome 
within the POD? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. Town Council decisions impacting the PDD ordinance have a reasonably foreseeable 
and material effect on Mayor Coler' s financial interest in her townhome. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
File No. A-19-016 
Page No. 2 
Barbara Coler is a duly elected Councilmember and Mayor of the Town of Fairfax. In Marin 
County, Fairfax is comprised of approximately 2.2 square miles, with a population of 7,607. Mayor 
Coler owns a townhome within a complex located in the Town. Aside from her ownership interest, 
Mayor Coler also has an exclusive easement for vehicular storage, and a non-exclusive easement 
for the use of the common area within the complex. Mayor Coler's property is within a 
homeowners' association ("HOA"), and there are covenants, conditions, and restrictions on the 
property. 
The Town will decide to adopt an ordinance amending its Zoning Code to set forth objective 
criteria for the Planned Development District ("PDD"). The undeveloped portions of the PDD zone 
consist of steeply sloped land that have a host of development challenges, including site stability 
and drainage. The ordinance would create development standards for residential density based on 
the slope within the PDD. The most steeply sloped sites would be more restricted in the amount of 
residential development that could occur, and the least steeply sloped sites would have fewer 
restrictions. 
The complex, including Mayor Coler's residence, is located within the PDD zone. 
Additionally, a PDD parcel is located next to a vacant, open space parcel that is also in the PDD 
zone and owned by the HOA. Pursuant to the Articles of Incorporation for the complex, each unit 
owner and lot owner has a right and easement of enjoyment to the common area that passes with the 
title to each unit and lot. The previous owner of the complex granted an easement to the Town over 
a portion of this open space parcel. The easement prohibits the construction of structures or 
improvements on the property encumbered by the easement. While the easement does not appear to 
cover the entire open space, the HOA appears to be disinterested in developing the portion of the 
open space property that is unencumbered by the easement. If the Town Council adopts the PDD 
zoning ordinance, the vacant open space parcel, as well as Mayor Coler's property, would be 
subject to the PDD zoning ordinance. 
Based on their review of the Town's parcels, the Town's staff estimates that there are 
approximately 285 parcels under PDD zoning. The Town has approximately 3,109 parcels. 
ANALYSIS 
Conflict of Interest: 
The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from making, participating 
in making, or using his or her official position in any way to influence a governmental decision in 
which the official knows, or has reason to know, that he or she has a "financial interest." (Section 
87100.) A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable 
from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or 
on any one of five enumerated economic interests. (Section 87103.) Under your facts, the applicable 
financial interest here is real property in which Mayor Coler has an interest worth $2,000 or more. 
(Section 87103(b).) 


Foreseeability and Materiality. 
File No. A-19-016 
Page No. 3 
A financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable when it is explicitly involved in 
a decision. Financial interests that are explicitly involved include an interest that is a named party 
in, or subject of, a government decision. An interest in real property is the subject of the decision 
and explicitly involved in the decision anytime the decision affects the property as described in 
Regulation 18702.2(a)(l)-(6). (Regulation 18701(a).) 
Providing the materiality standard for real property explicitly involved in a decision, 
Regulation 18702.2(a) states in pertinent part, a reasonably foreseeable effect is material if the 
decision: 
(1) Involves the adoption of or amendment to a development plan or criteria 
applying to the parcel 
(2) Determines the parcel's zoning or rezoning ( other than a zoning decision 
applicable to all properties designated in that category), annexation or de-
annexation, or inclusion in or exclusion from any city, county, district, or other 
local government subdivision, or other boundaries, other than elective district 
boundaries as determined by the California Citizen's Redistricting Commission or 
any other agency where the governmental decision is to determine boundaries for 
elective purposes.2 
Mayor Coler seeks advice regarding her potential involvement in a Town Council decision 
to adopt an ordinance establishing residential density development criteria for a zoning area in 
which her townhome lies. This decision involves the adoption of or amendment to a development 
plan or criteria applying to Mayor Coler's property. Based upon the facts provided, Mayor Coler's 
financial interest is explicitly involved in the decision and the effect of the decision is both 
foreseeable under Regulation 18702.2(a) and material under Regulation 18702.2(a)(l). Thus, 
Mayor Coler may not make, participate in making, or influence the decision. 
Public Generally Exception. 
Regulation 18703 provides: 
"(a) General Rule. A _governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's 
financial interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the 
official establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected anci the effect 
on his or her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on the 
significant segment." 
2 In this case, the ordinance changes are not applicable to all properties within the PDD zone. Although the 
Town's Zoning Code will be amended to set forth objective criteria, the ordinance would create development standards 
for residential density based on the slope of the individual property. 


File No. A-19-016 
Page No. 4 
A significant segment of the public, as pertinent to your facts, is at least 25 percent of "[a]ll 
real property, commercial real property, or residential real property within the official's 
jurisdiction." 
You indicated that the impacted PDD comprises approximately 9% of the Town's parcels. 
This percentage falls short of establishing a "significant segment" of the official's jurisdiction. 
Regulation 18703 also contains several enumerated special circumstances in which the exception 
may apply. However, none of the special circumstances apply based upon your facts. The special 
circumstance for small jurisdictions set forth in former Regulation 18707 .10 was repealed on 
June 17, 2015. Regulation 18703 was amended to incorporate the current public generally 
exception. Accordingly, Mayor Coler has a disqualifying conflict of interest and must follow proper 
disqualification procedure; (See, e.g., Regulation 18707.) 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
RPOC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Ryan O'Connor 
Counsel, Legal Division

Does Mayor Coler have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act in participating in a

objective criteria for the Planned Development District ("PPD"), when she owns a townhome 
within the POD?

and material effect on Mayor Coler' s financial interest in her townhome.

Town Council decision to adopt an ordinance amending the Town's Zoning Code to set forth 
objective criteria for the Planned Development District ("PPD"), when she owns a townhome 
within the POD?

CONCLUSION: Yes. Town Council decisions impacting the PDD ordinance have a reasonably foreseeable 
and material effect on Mayor Coler' s financial interest in her townhome.
